one_line_takeaway: MORE is the first white-box attack to jointly wreck ASR accuracy and inference efficiency, using a hierarchical repulsion-anchoring scheme plus a repetition-doubling loss (REDO) to make Whisper emit wrong transcripts up to ~10x longer than prior attacks.
key_finding: MORE produces dramatically longer transcriptions than all baselines while keeping WER competitively high. On LibriSpeech at 35 dB it forces ~10x longer outputs than accuracy-only attacks and ~3.8x longer than SlothSpeech (the only prior efficiency attack). On the robust Whisper-large model (LibriSpeech, 35 dB) MORE reaches WER 53.72 and length 301.47, versus SlothSpeech's WER 34.21 / length 79.78 and accuracy baselines around WER 30 with lengths near clean (~21). Ablations show every component matters: removing REDO collapses length from 296.66 to 120.67, removing the accuracy loss collapses WER from 90.85 to 27.58 (length preserved), and removing both efficiency components (EOS+REDO) collapses length to 30.60. Lower SNR (30 dB, noisier) yields stronger attacks. A case study shows MORE emitting 'and her voice' ~100 times (length 334, WER 100).
core_contribution: MORE (Multi-Objective Repetitive Doubling Encouragement) is a white-box adversarial attack on large-scale ASR models (the Whisper family) that jointly degrades BOTH recognition accuracy AND inference efficiency from a single perturbed audio input. The paper's own novelty (distinct from the Related Work it cites) is: (a) the first attack unifying accuracy-degradation and efficiency-degradation objectives against ASR via a hierarchical two-stage 'repulsion-anchoring' optimization strategy, motivated by the observation that broad accuracy gradients and sharp, EOS-concentrated efficiency gradients destabilize each other if optimized jointly in one step; and (b) REDO (Repetitive Encouragement Doubling Objective), a novel loss that exploits transformer repetition loops by periodically constructing a doubled target sequence (repeated decoded segment) and using cross-entropy to force the model into structured, semantically-coherent repetition, thereby inflating output length. The efficiency stage combines REDO with an EOS-suppression loss (penalize the EOS logit while boosting the second-most-likely token) and an 'asymmetric interleaving' curriculum that extends the repeated target only every D steps to stabilize long-horizon optimization.
threat_model: White-box attack with full gradient access to the victim ASR model. Attacker crafts an additive perturbation delta on the raw waveform (X+delta), constrained for imperceptibility by both an SNR/energy bound and an L-infinity peak-amplitude bound (epsilon = ||X||_inf / SNR), aligned with psychoacoustic masking. Goal is a single adversarial input that simultaneously (i) maximizes WER relative to ground truth and (ii) maximizes predicted-sequence length, inducing incorrect transcriptions at greatly elevated compute cost (a denial-of-service / responsiveness attack on real-time ASR such as captioning, clinical dictation, navigation). The paper's ethics statement notes attacks were only run on public research datasets, not deployed systems, and proposes defenses (repetition/loop detectors at decode time, input band-limiting, adversarial training targeting EOS/repetition pathologies).
limitations: Purely white-box with full gradient access; no black-box or transferability evaluation (contrasts with the paper's own citation of transferable ASR attacks). Attack surface is limited to the Whisper encoder-decoder family and to two clean read-speech datasets (LibriSpeech, LJ-Speech) with only the first 500 utterances each; no noisy/streaming/real-world audio. WER is truncated to reference length and capped at 100%, which measures only the initial portion of very long adversarial outputs. Imperceptibility is argued via SNR/L-infinity bounds and cited psychoacoustic principles rather than a human listening study. No over-the-air / physical-channel test (perturbations are added digitally). Defenses are proposed but not implemented or evaluated. Code withheld ('proprietary requirements'), to be released only upon acceptance.

quantitative_results: Whisper-large, LibriSpeech, 35 dB: MORE WER 53.72, length 301.47 vs SlothSpeech WER 34.21 / length 79.78 (~3.8x longer than SlothSpeech, ~10x longer than accuracy baselines whose length ~21)
Whisper-base, LibriSpeech, 35 dB: MORE length 300.13 vs PGD length 31.65
Whisper-small, LJ-Speech, 35 dB: MORE length 208.51 vs SlothSpeech length 65.85

Ablation (LJ-Speech, 35 dB): full MORE WER 90.85 / length 296.66; remove REDO -> length 120.67 (WER 92.42); remove accuracy loss -> WER 27.58 (length 293.03); remove EOS+REDO -> length 30.60 (WER 93.63); remove EOS -> length 233.84; remove REDO+Lacc -> WER 47.10 / length 116.05
Case study: MORE forces Whisper to repeat 'and her voice' ~100 times, length 334, WER 100.00 (clean reference length 12)
SNR levels: 35 dB (eps=0.002) and 30 dB (eps=0.0035); 30 dB yields higher WER and longer outputs
